title: Lamingtons
subtitle: Australian sponge squares coated in chocolate icing and desiccated coconut.
excerpt: Lamingtons are squares of plain sponge, dipped in a thin chocolate icing and rolled in desiccated coconut. They are an Australian national cake, found at every school fete and morning tea from Queensland to Victoria. The sponge must be day-old and very cold before dipping, or it will fall apart in the coating. Makes about 16 pieces.
sourceNotes: Lamingtons are named after Lord Lamington, Governor of Queensland from 1896 to 1901. The recipe appeared in Queensland newspapers and recipe books by the early twentieth century. The standard account credits the cake to his chef, though the origin is contested. By mid-century they were firmly established across Australia and New Zealand as a bake sale staple. The desiccated-coconut-and-chocolate coating method has remained unchanged since those early recipes.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze coated lamingtons in a single layer, then transfer to a bag. Defrost at room temperature for an hour.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make and refrigerate the sponge a day ahead; cold sponge is essential for dipping without crumbling.

recipeTools.notes: 20 cm square tin, greased and lined.
Or a hand mixer for the sponge batter.
Set over a tray to catch the drips during dipping.

content.text: A lamington starts as a very plain sponge cake, baked in a square tin, then left to chill overnight. The chilling is not optional: a warm or even room-temperature sponge disintegrates the moment it hits the warm chocolate icing. Cold cake holds together. Cut into 16 squares, dip each one twice, roll in coconut, and set on a rack.
The icing should be the consistency of thick cream: thin enough to coat but not so thin it runs straight off. Make it, test it, thin with a little hot water if needed.
Heat the oven to 170°C fan (190°C conventional). Grease and line a 20 cm square tin.
Beat {{unsalted-butter}} and {{caster-sugar}} together until pale and fluffy, about 3 minutes. Beat in {{eggs}} one at a time, then add {{vanilla-extract}}.
Fold in {{self-raising-flour}} in two additions, alternating with {{whole-milk}}, until just combined. Spread evenly into the tin.

Bake for 22 to 25 minutes until risen, golden, and springy. Cool in the tin for 10 minutes, then turn onto a rack. When completely cool, wrap in cling film and refrigerate for at least 2 hours, or overnight.
Cut the cold sponge into 16 equal squares using a sharp knife. Keep in the fridge while you make the icing.

Sift {{icing-sugar}} and {{cocoa-powder}} into a bowl. Add {{unsalted-butter}} and {{whole-milk}}. Stir until smooth and the consistency of thick pouring cream. If too thick, add hot water a teaspoon at a time.

Spread {{desiccated-coconut}} on a flat plate. Set a cooling rack over a tray. Working quickly with cold cake squares, dip each piece in the chocolate icing using two forks, turning to coat all sides. Allow excess to drip off for a few seconds, then roll in the coconut. Place on the rack to set. Repeat.
Leave on the rack for 30 minutes to firm up before serving. The icing will set; the coconut will stick. Store in a single layer in a tin at room temperature for up to three days.

attrs.heading: Troubleshooting
attrs.intro: Lamington problems almost always come from the sponge temperature.

items.fix: Refrigerate for at least 2 hours, ideally overnight. Even a slightly warm sponge will crumble into the icing.

items.symptom: Icing too thick and clumping on the sponge.
items.cause: Not enough hot milk in the icing.
items.fix: Thin the icing by adding hot water a teaspoon at a time until it coats the back of a spoon but flows freely when tipped.
